ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for pickled okra with bonito flakes; closest defensible match is the generic pickled vegetable food product class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pickled vegetables (tsukemono-type); closest FoodEx2 anchor for salt- or vinegar-pickled vegetable preparations
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for pickled okra with bonito flakes; A00LC (pickled vegetables) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for pickled okra; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side serving of pickled okra with bonito flakes is approximately 60 g, equivalent to 4–6 whole pickled okra pods garnished with a pinch of dried bonito flakes.

nutrient_source.title: Pickled Okra — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for Pickled Okra (cr_id G00010597) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated at 0.04 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central entry for raw okra (FDC ID 169260), scaled proportionally to the low total fat content of 0.17 g/100 g. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported by HPB or USDA for this pickled preparation; set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Vegetables — Analogous estimate based on pickled/vinegar-treated low-GI vegetables; okra GI reference from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pickled okra with bonito flakes in the University of Sydney GI Database. Okra is consistently classified as a low-GI vegetable (GI approximately 20) in peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283). Vinegar in pickling brine is documented to further attenuate postprandial glucose response (Johnston CS et al., 2004, JADA). GI estimated at 20 (low). GL per 60 g serving = (20 × 11.04 × 0.60) / 100 = 1.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 950 mg/100 g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit pickled okra due to its very high sodium content of 950 mg/100 g; rinsing under cold water before consumption can reduce surface brine sodium, and portion size should not exceed 30 g per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~20), mucilaginous dietary fibre 2.7 g/100 g slows glucose absorption; sugar 6.1 g/100 g does not trigger the diabetes hard gate
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pickled okra is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions due to its low glycemic index and fibre content, though the high sodium load warrants caution and portion control; vinegar in the pickling brine may additionally attenuate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 950 mg/100 g poses significant cardiovascular risk; fat content is negligible at 0.17 g/100 g with no saturated fat concern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Despite its very low fat content, pickled okra's extremely high sodium content of 950 mg/100 g makes it unsuitable for regular consumption by individuals with cardiovascular disease; it should be treated as an occasional condiment in portions of 20–30 g maximum.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 950 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g RA threshold, promoting fluid retention and joint swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid pickled okra due to its very high sodium content of 950 mg/100 g, which can worsen joint inflammation and tissue swelling; fresh or steamed okra is a suitable low-sodium alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pickled Okra with Bonito Flakes (酢漬けオクラ、かつお節添え; Suzuke Okura) is a Japanese-style pickled vegetable side dish consisting of whole or sliced okra pods preserved in a salt-and-vinegar brine and garnished with dried bonito flakes (katsuobushi). The pickling process gives the okra a crisp, slightly tangy texture while retaining its characteristic mucilaginous interior. Bonito flakes add a delicate umami savouriness. The dish is served chilled or at room temperature as a tsukemono-style accompaniment to rice meals or as a small appetiser. Nutritionally, pickled okra provides modest energy (48 kcal/100 g), low fat (0.17 g/100 g), moderate carbohydrate (11.04 g/100 g), and useful dietary fibre (2.7 g/100 g). Its most clinically significant feature is its very high sodium content of 950 mg/100 g, derived from the salt-heavy pickling brine, which makes it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis in any meaningful portion. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the food is low-GI (estimated GI ~20) and the mucilaginous fibre in okra slows glucose absorption; vinegar in the brine may further reduce postprandial glucose response. Calcium (70 mg/100 g), magnesium (47 mg/100 g), and potassium (248 mg/100 g) are present in useful amounts. The bonito flakes introduce a fish allergen and render the dish non-vegetarian and non-vegan. This dish is commonly consumed in Japanese cuisine and is available in Singapore as part of Japanese restaurant menus.
common_user_queries: Is pickled okra good for diabetes?
Can I eat pickled okra if I have high blood pressure?
How much sodium is in pickled okra?
What is the glycemic index of okra?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo database (cr_id G00010597) with a reported confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central raw okra data scaled to the reported total fat. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous food evidence for okra and peer-reviewed literature on vinegar's glycaemic effect. All mandatory Japanese-language naming requirements are satisfied.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.04 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central raw okra (FDC ID 169260) proportionally scaled to the HPB-reported total fat of 0.17 g/100 g; HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, full vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this pickled okra preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source; okra is a plant food and bonito flakes contribute negligible cholesterol at typical garnish quantities). 4. Food group corrected from input 'Grains and staples / Baking ingredients' to 'Vegetables / Pickled vegetables' — the input classification was clearly erroneous for a pickled vegetable dish. 5. GI is an analogous estimate (~20, low) based on University of Sydney GI Database okra data and peer-reviewed literature; no direct GI measurement for pickled okra with bonito flakes exists in the approved GI database. GL per 60 g serving = 1.3. 6. The description in the input record ('Crisp pickled okra with savory bonito flakes') and the cultural classification as Japanese cuisine are preserved. 7. Bonito flakes (katsuobushi) are a fish-derived ingredient; the dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans and contains a fish allergen.
